database ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|lt=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県南相馬市原町区高城ノ内112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Taka-jinja}} [[JA:多珂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430895}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Taka Shrine |Image = [[File:Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine in Shinto ritual season 1.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}112 Takajō-no-uchi |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2 DMS|37|36|2.81|N|140|59|33.52|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=ti|name=Taka Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} (Taka Ara-Mitama-no-Mikoto) |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = 40th year of Emperor Keikō (12th Emperor), 7th month |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = Taka Daimyōjin |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Taka Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taka Shrine''' (たかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank}} it was designated as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined Deity === * '''{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}''' *: According to *Ōsōshi*, a local historical record of the Sōma Domain, the shrine enshrines “Taka Ara-Mitama-no-Mikoto,” the {{ill|lt=Ara-mitama and Nigi-mitama|Q495568|WD=Q495568|lt=Ara-mitama}} (rough spirit) of Izanagi-no-Mikoto. The shintai (sacred object) has been prohibited from being viewed since ancient times and is therefore unknown. There is a theory that it may be a “hawk statue,” but this hawk statue is said to be the result of a mistaken transmission identifying the “White-Token Hawk” mentioned in shrine tradition as the shintai. === History === === Overview === Taka Shrine is the foremost of the eight {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Namekata District, Fukushima|Q11626139|WD=Q11626139}}, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389}} (Taka Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}}, {{ill|lt=Mitachi Shrine|Q11488802|WD=Q11488802}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masutamine Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himatsuri Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takakura Shrine|Q11669904|WD=Q11669904}}, {{ill|lt=Kanmuri Mine Shrine|Q11395049|WD=Q11395049}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oshio Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}). Among shrines bearing the name “Taga Shrine,” it is the only one nationwide to have been granted the rank of {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}, and it is said that Taga Shrines throughout the Tōhoku region originate from this shrine. Although its exact origins are unknown, tradition holds that the shrine was founded in the 40th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q461258}}, who visited this area during his eastern campaign and enshrined the deity to pray for victory in battle. To the northwest of the current location lies the Furuuchi area, through which the Ōta River flows, and there is said to be a place called “Daimyōjin Kawara.” The ancient shrine site is said to have been on the southern hills of Daimyōjin Kawara. In the 7th year, 2nd month of {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}, a violent storm washed away and severely damaged the shrine buildings, which drifted to the banks of the nearby Ōta River. In the 9th month of the same year, the shrine was relocated to Ashinodaira (present-day Jō-no-uchi, the current site). In Kōryaku 2 ({{ill|lt=1380|Q6327|WD=Q6327}}), there existed a bronze bell (waniguchi) bearing an inscription of donation by Fujiwara Yoshimori, which is said to hang in the inner sanctuary of the main hall. From April of Jōō 4, Kinoe-mi year ({{ill|lt=1655|Q6975|WD=Q6975}}) to Meireki 1 (the same year), the shrine sanctuary was repaired, with timber donated by {{ill|lt=Sōma Tadatane|Q8514579|WD=Q8514579}}. During the {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} era, {{ill|lt=Sōma Masatane|Q8514599|WD=Q8514599}} built a sanctuary called “Yōshinden,” associated with {{ill|lt=Yoshida Shintō|Q1760351|WD=Q1760351}}, within the castle grounds, and at that time, for certain reasons, had a statue of the “White-Token Hawk” carved and dedicated to Taka Shrine. This wooden statue of the “White-Token Hawk” is said to have been stored in a miniature shrine. Even today, the “White-Token Hawk Statue” is enshrined in the main hall as a sacred treasure. According to shrine tradition, although it appears to be an ordinary wooden statue, it was named “White-Token Hawk” because the feathers appear white depending on how the light strikes them. In Kyōhō 9 ({{ill|lt=1724|Q6926|WD=Q6926}}), Sōma Masatane donated shrine lands amounting to 1 koku and 9 to, among other offerings, and the shrine received deep reverence from successive lords of the Sōma Domain. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was designated a village shrine, and in Shōwa 19 ({{ill|lt=1944|Q5268|WD=Q5268}}), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine. Taka Shrine has long been believed to bestow divine benefits such as longevity, fulfillment of wishes, household safety, safe childbirth, maritime safety, and abundant catches. Because it was originally enshrined to pray for victory in battle, many worshippers in particular visit to pray for certain victory in elections. === Court Rank === * Enryaku 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452}}) 10th month, 22nd day: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Kōki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}*) – recorded as “Taga Shrine.” === Shrine Grounds === === Shrine Buildings === * Main Hall – Built in one-bay nagare-zukuri style, with side screens and balustrades. Dragon carvings are applied to the side screens. File:Honden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Main Hall File:Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine in Shinto ritual season 2.JPG|Worship Hall '''Interior of the Heiden''' The coffered lattice ceiling of the heiden is decorated with polychrome paintings of animals, plants, and human figures. Only during the first three days of the New Year, a goma altar is set up in front of the main hall doors within the heiden, where goma fire rituals are performed. During these rituals, prayers are offered by holding amulets and votive items directly over the sacred flames. File:Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Interior of the Heiden File:Pictures of Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Paintings on the lattice ceiling of the Heiden
The rear of the photo shows the side of the main hall doors. File:Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Paintings on the lattice ceiling of the Heiden
The rear of the photo shows the Heiden entrance. File:Gomadan of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Goma altar at Taka Shrine
Only during the New Year period, a tree called “Nodeppō” is used as goma sticks for the ritual. '''Interior of the Worship Hall''' Inside the worship hall are housed items such as an ema depicting “Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Izanami-no-Mikoto,” paintings of hawks, and a plaque reading “Protection of the State,” dedicated in Meiji 38 to commemorate the {{ill|lt=Siege of Port Arthur|Q210800|WD=Q210800|lt=Fall of Port Arthur}} during the Russo-Japanese War. File:Inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine 1.JPG|Interior of the Worship Hall File:Inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Interior of the Worship Hall / Entrance to the Heiden File:Ema board of Izanagi and Izanami at Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Ema of Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Izanami-no-Mikoto File:Picture of Falcon inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Hawk painting
Many hawk images are dedicated because the shrine is also called “Taka Daimyōjin.” File:Board of "spiritual protection of the state" at Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|“Protection of the State” votive plaque === Other Structures === * Stone statues of the Seven Lucky Gods * Near the sacred tree behind the main hall, Buddhist statues from the era of syncretism between Shinto and Buddhism are placed. === Approach === Along the approach is a sacred pond, and on the small central island stand a small shrine of Kogota Yamatsumi Shrine and a stone monument of Konpira Shrine. The first torii gate stands southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Ōta Station|Q6100384|WD=Q6100384}}, along the tracks of the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263}}. File:Kogota Yamatsumi-jinja shrine and Konpira-jinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Small shrine of Kogota Yamatsumi Shrine and stone monument of Konpira Shrine File:Main approach to Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Approach File:Main approach to Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Entrance to the approach of Taka Shrine File:Torii gate of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|First torii gate === Subsidiary and Branch Shrines === * Yasaka-Inari Shrine *: The shrine building reuses the former main hall of Taka Shrine, and within it are enshrined Yasaka Shrine and Inari Shrine. Yasaka Shrine was formerly called Gozu Tennō Shrine and had been located in the Jō-no-uchi area, the present location. According to *Ōsōshi*, the sacred object of Yasaka Shrine measures 7 sun in length. Inari Shrine was formerly located in the Oinari area southeast of the current site and was later enshrined together with Yasaka Shrine. *: On the east side of the shrine building are a sacred cedar tree, Raijin Shrine, and a stone monument of Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine). * Raijin Shrine *: The sacred object is recorded as measuring 7 sun in length. * Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine) Stone Monument *: The enshrined deities are Amaterasu Ōmikami, Tsukuyomi-no-Kami, and Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami. It was transferred from {{ill|lt=Aomasa Shrine|Q11662833|WD=Q11662833}} in Iwakiri, Miyagino Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture. File:Yasaka-Inari-jinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Yasaka-Inari Shrine (former main hall of Taka Shrine) File:Sanctified Japanese cedar tree and raijinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Sacred tree and Raijin Shrine File:Raijinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Raijin Shrine File:Aoso-jinja shrine in taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone monument of Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine) File:Statues of Shichifukujin in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone statues of the Seven Lucky Gods File:Sanctified tree and Statue of Kannon in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Sacred tree and Buddhist statue === Kifune Shrine (Ronsha of Taka Shrine) === {{Shrine |Name = Kifune Shrine |Image = [[File:Haiden of Kifune-jinja shrine in Odaka ward,Minamisouma city. 01.JPG|270px]]

{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} (trad.)
{{ill|lt=Takami-Musubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} (trad.)
Koyō Myōjin (trad.) |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} |Founded = Unknown (before Genkō 3 ({{ill|lt=1323|Q5771|WD=Q5771}})) |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Annual festival = Second Sunday of January |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kifune Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E|}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=290|coord={{coord|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E}}}} }} It is enshrined near Sōma Odaka Shrine in Odaka Ward, Minamisōma City. The enshrined deities are Takaokami-no-Kami and Yamiokami-no-Kami. === History === The founding date of Kifune Shrine is unknown, but it is said to have been worshipped since before Genkō 3 (1323). According to *Ōsōshi*, there is a record stating that the box housing the sacred object bears an inscription indicating it was made in the 5th year of Hōen, kinoe-mi year, 8th month. The sacred object is said to be a statue “riding a dragon and wearing white robes.” In Bunji 5, when {{ill|lt=Sōma Moritsune|Q11582495|WD=Q11582495}} took control of Namekata District, he designated Kifune Shrine as the district’s chief shrine and deeply revered it, carrying out embellishments of the shrine buildings. Kifune Shrine was mainly worshipped as a deity of rainmaking and stopping rain, bountiful harvests, success and advancement in life, and martial arts. According to *Ōsōshi*, Taka Shrine once existed within the precincts of Kibune (Kifune) Shrine in Minami-Odaka Village. It was a shrine building measuring 2 shaku 5 sun in depth. It was a shrine enshrined during the era of Emperor Keikō, with Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto as the two main deities, and Koyō Myōjin as an accompanying deity. In Bunka 12 (1815), the lord of the Sōma Domain, {{ill|lt=Sōma Masutane|Q8514605|WD=Q8514605}}, ordered shrine priests and a man named Watanabe Yoshitsuna to investigate and identify the eight Shikinaisha in Namekata District. Watanabe Yoshitsuna reported that the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha known as Taka Shrine was the Taka Daimyōjin enshrined within the grounds of Kōmyō-ji Temple in Taka Village. In response, Takadama Tanba, a shrine priest of the Kibune Shrine in Odaka Village, submitted a counter-report claiming that Taka Shrine was a small shrine enshrined in Odaka Village. According to that report, Taka Shrine was a shrine where the Yasakani no Magatama, one of the Three Sacred Treasures, was enshrined as the local tutelary deity, and it had long been the guardian deity of Odaka Village. The ancestors of the shrine priest family originally bore the name “Takadama,” but out of deference to Taka Shrine they changed it to “Takadama.” The Taga faith’s head shrine, {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091}} in Shiga Prefecture, is called “O-Taga-sama,” and the name Odaka is said to derive from calling the enshrined Taka Shrine “Mi-Taka.” The Takadama family administered the rites of both Kifune Shrine and Taka Shrine, which were enshrined near {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōma Odaka Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in Odaka Ward. Taka Shrine was located at a place called Nihonmatsu in Odaka Village, about ten ken from Kifune Shrine. Later, Taka Shrine was washed away by a flood during the Genroku era, but a farmer recovered the small shrine from the water and it was enshrined together within the precincts of Kifune Shrine. Takadama Tanba wrote down the above origins of Taka Shrine in Odaka Village and reported them to the domain. However, according to the compiler of *Ōsōshi*: * In shrine identification surveys conducted before the domain’s Bunka 12 investigation, Taka Shrine of Odaka Village is not recorded. * Records of the Shima family, shrine priests of Taka Shrine in Taka Village, appear in shrine priest registries from Genroku 15 and in temple and shrine surveys from the Shōtoku era, whereas records of the Takadama family only appear from Kanpō 1 onward. For these reasons, the theory of Takadama Tanba is said to contain many doubtful points. At present, the inscription on the stone monument describing the shrine’s origins at the entrance of Kifune Shrine’s approach states that the enshrined deities are Takaokami-no-Kami and Yamiokami-no-Kami, and that Taka Shrine is enshrined together in the same sanctuary. === Shrine Grounds === File:Precincts of Kifune-jinja,Minamisoma.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Haiden of Kifune-jinja after massive earthquake 11 March 2011.JPG|Worship Hall File:Honden of Kifune-jinja after massive earthquake 11 March 2011.JPG|Main Hall
Nagare-zukuri style. === Related Sites === In the Sōma region, there are said to be five Taka Shrines in total, including the two shrines described above. File:Taka-jinja shrine at Suzumigaoka Hachiman-jinja shrine.JPG|Taka Shrine, a precinct shrine within Suzumigaoka Hachiman Shrine in Sōma City === On-site Information === '''Locations''' * Taka Shrine: {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}112 Takajō-no-uchi * Kifune Shrine: {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Odaka Ward|Q24892654|WD=Q24892654}}2-50 Nakamachi === References === * Guide booklet for visiting Taka Shrine (Taka Shrine Office) * *Ōsōshi (History of Sōma City, Vol. 4)* (Sōma City, 1969) === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External Links === * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/276003.html Taka Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Compilation”) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Taka Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Taka Shrine (Minamisōma)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Taka Shrine (Minamisōma)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:南相馬市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社|名たかしんしや]] [[Category:福島県の旧県社]] [[Category:南相馬市の建築物]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

Worship Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}112 Takajō-no-uchi |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2 DMS|37|36|2.81|N|140|59|33.52|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=ti|name=Taka Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} (Taka Ara-Mitama-no-Mikoto) |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = 40th year of Emperor Keikō (12th Emperor), 7th month |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = Taka Daimyōjin |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Taka Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taka Shrine''' (たかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank}} it was designated as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined Deity === * '''{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}''' *: According to *Ōsōshi*, a local historical record of the Sōma Domain, the shrine enshrines “Taka Ara-Mitama-no-Mikoto,” the {{ill|lt=Ara-mitama and Nigi-mitama|Q495568|WD=Q495568|lt=Ara-mitama}} (rough spirit) of Izanagi-no-Mikoto. The shintai (sacred object) has been prohibited from being viewed since ancient times and is therefore unknown. There is a theory that it may be a “hawk statue,” but this hawk statue is said to be the result of a mistaken transmission identifying the “White-Token Hawk” mentioned in shrine tradition as the shintai. === History === === Overview === Taka Shrine is the foremost of the eight {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Namekata District, Fukushima|Q11626139|WD=Q11626139}}, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389}} (Taka Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}}, {{ill|lt=Mitachi Shrine|Q11488802|WD=Q11488802}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masutamine Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himatsuri Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takakura Shrine|Q11669904|WD=Q11669904}}, {{ill|lt=Kanmuri Mine Shrine|Q11395049|WD=Q11395049}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oshio Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}). Among shrines bearing the name “Taga Shrine,” it is the only one nationwide to have been granted the rank of {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}, and it is said that Taga Shrines throughout the Tōhoku region originate from this shrine. Although its exact origins are unknown, tradition holds that the shrine was founded in the 40th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q461258}}, who visited this area during his eastern campaign and enshrined the deity to pray for victory in battle. To the northwest of the current location lies the Furuuchi area, through which the Ōta River flows, and there is said to be a place called “Daimyōjin Kawara.” The ancient shrine site is said to have been on the southern hills of Daimyōjin Kawara. In the 7th year, 2nd month of {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}, a violent storm washed away and severely damaged the shrine buildings, which drifted to the banks of the nearby Ōta River. In the 9th month of the same year, the shrine was relocated to Ashinodaira (present-day Jō-no-uchi, the current site). In Kōryaku 2 ({{ill|lt=1380|Q6327|WD=Q6327}}), there existed a bronze bell (waniguchi) bearing an inscription of donation by Fujiwara Yoshimori, which is said to hang in the inner sanctuary of the main hall. From April of Jōō 4, Kinoe-mi year ({{ill|lt=1655|Q6975|WD=Q6975}}) to Meireki 1 (the same year), the shrine sanctuary was repaired, with timber donated by {{ill|lt=Sōma Tadatane|Q8514579|WD=Q8514579}}. During the {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} era, {{ill|lt=Sōma Masatane|Q8514599|WD=Q8514599}} built a sanctuary called “Yōshinden,” associated with {{ill|lt=Yoshida Shintō|Q1760351|WD=Q1760351}}, within the castle grounds, and at that time, for certain reasons, had a statue of the “White-Token Hawk” carved and dedicated to Taka Shrine. This wooden statue of the “White-Token Hawk” is said to have been stored in a miniature shrine. Even today, the “White-Token Hawk Statue” is enshrined in the main hall as a sacred treasure. According to shrine tradition, although it appears to be an ordinary wooden statue, it was named “White-Token Hawk” because the feathers appear white depending on how the light strikes them. In Kyōhō 9 ({{ill|lt=1724|Q6926|WD=Q6926}}), Sōma Masatane donated shrine lands amounting to 1 koku and 9 to, among other offerings, and the shrine received deep reverence from successive lords of the Sōma Domain. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was designated a village shrine, and in Shōwa 19 ({{ill|lt=1944|Q5268|WD=Q5268}}), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine. Taka Shrine has long been believed to bestow divine benefits such as longevity, fulfillment of wishes, household safety, safe childbirth, maritime safety, and abundant catches. Because it was originally enshrined to pray for victory in battle, many worshippers in particular visit to pray for certain victory in elections. === Court Rank === * Enryaku 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452}}) 10th month, 22nd day: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Kōki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}*) – recorded as “Taga Shrine.” === Shrine Grounds === === Shrine Buildings === * Main Hall – Built in one-bay nagare-zukuri style, with side screens and balustrades. Dragon carvings are applied to the side screens. File:Honden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Main Hall File:Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine in Shinto ritual season 2.JPG|Worship Hall '''Interior of the Heiden''' The coffered lattice ceiling of the heiden is decorated with polychrome paintings of animals, plants, and human figures. Only during the first three days of the New Year, a goma altar is set up in front of the main hall doors within the heiden, where goma fire rituals are performed. During these rituals, prayers are offered by holding amulets and votive items directly over the sacred flames. File:Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Interior of the Heiden File:Pictures of Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Paintings on the lattice ceiling of the Heiden
The rear of the photo shows the side of the main hall doors. File:Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Paintings on the lattice ceiling of the Heiden
The rear of the photo shows the Heiden entrance. File:Gomadan of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Goma altar at Taka Shrine
Only during the New Year period, a tree called “Nodeppō” is used as goma sticks for the ritual. '''Interior of the Worship Hall''' Inside the worship hall are housed items such as an ema depicting “Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Izanami-no-Mikoto,” paintings of hawks, and a plaque reading “Protection of the State,” dedicated in Meiji 38 to commemorate the {{ill|lt=Siege of Port Arthur|Q210800|WD=Q210800|lt=Fall of Port Arthur}} during the Russo-Japanese War. File:Inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine 1.JPG|Interior of the Worship Hall File:Inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Interior of the Worship Hall / Entrance to the Heiden File:Ema board of Izanagi and Izanami at Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Ema of Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Izanami-no-Mikoto File:Picture of Falcon inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Hawk painting
Many hawk images are dedicated because the shrine is also called “Taka Daimyōjin.” File:Board of "spiritual protection of the state" at Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|“Protection of the State” votive plaque === Other Structures === * Stone statues of the Seven Lucky Gods * Near the sacred tree behind the main hall, Buddhist statues from the era of syncretism between Shinto and Buddhism are placed. === Approach === Along the approach is a sacred pond, and on the small central island stand a small shrine of Kogota Yamatsumi Shrine and a stone monument of Konpira Shrine. The first torii gate stands southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Ōta Station|Q6100384|WD=Q6100384}}, along the tracks of the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263}}. File:Kogota Yamatsumi-jinja shrine and Konpira-jinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Small shrine of Kogota Yamatsumi Shrine and stone monument of Konpira Shrine File:Main approach to Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Approach File:Main approach to Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Entrance to the approach of Taka Shrine File:Torii gate of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|First torii gate === Subsidiary and Branch Shrines === * Yasaka-Inari Shrine *: The shrine building reuses the former main hall of Taka Shrine, and within it are enshrined Yasaka Shrine and Inari Shrine. Yasaka Shrine was formerly called Gozu Tennō Shrine and had been located in the Jō-no-uchi area, the present location. According to *Ōsōshi*, the sacred object of Yasaka Shrine measures 7 sun in length. Inari Shrine was formerly located in the Oinari area southeast of the current site and was later enshrined together with Yasaka Shrine. *: On the east side of the shrine building are a sacred cedar tree, Raijin Shrine, and a stone monument of Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine). * Raijin Shrine *: The sacred object is recorded as measuring 7 sun in length. * Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine) Stone Monument *: The enshrined deities are Amaterasu Ōmikami, Tsukuyomi-no-Kami, and Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami. It was transferred from {{ill|lt=Aomasa Shrine|Q11662833|WD=Q11662833}} in Iwakiri, Miyagino Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture. File:Yasaka-Inari-jinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Yasaka-Inari Shrine (former main hall of Taka Shrine) File:Sanctified Japanese cedar tree and raijinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Sacred tree and Raijin Shrine File:Raijinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Raijin Shrine File:Aoso-jinja shrine in taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone monument of Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine) File:Statues of Shichifukujin in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone statues of the Seven Lucky Gods File:Sanctified tree and Statue of Kannon in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Sacred tree and Buddhist statue === Kifune Shrine (Ronsha of Taka Shrine) === {{Shrine |Name = Kifune Shrine |Image = [[File:Haiden of Kifune-jinja shrine in Odaka ward,Minamisouma city. 01.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Odaka Ward|Q24892654|WD=Q24892654}}2-50 Nakamachi |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2 DMS|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|name=Kifune Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami / Yamiokami-no-Kami}}
{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} (trad.)
{{ill|lt=Takami-Musubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} (trad.)
Koyō Myōjin (trad.) |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} |Founded = Unknown (before Genkō 3 ({{ill|lt=1323|Q5771|WD=Q5771}})) |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Annual festival = Second Sunday of January |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kifune Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E|}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=290|coord={{coord|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E}}}} }} It is enshrined near Sōma Odaka Shrine in Odaka Ward, Minamisōma City. The enshrined deities are Takaokami-no-Kami and Yamiokami-no-Kami. === History === The founding date of Kifune Shrine is unknown, but it is said to have been worshipped since before Genkō 3 (1323). According to *Ōsōshi*, there is a record stating that the box housing the sacred object bears an inscription indicating it was made in the 5th year of Hōen, kinoe-mi year, 8th month. The sacred object is said to be a statue “riding a dragon and wearing white robes.” In Bunji 5, when {{ill|lt=Sōma Moritsune|Q11582495|WD=Q11582495}} took control of Namekata District, he designated Kifune Shrine as the district’s chief shrine and deeply revered it, carrying out embellishments of the shrine buildings. Kifune Shrine was mainly worshipped as a deity of rainmaking and stopping rain, bountiful harvests, success and advancement in life, and martial arts. According to *Ōsōshi*, Taka Shrine once existed within the precincts of Kibune (Kifune) Shrine in Minami-Odaka Village. It was a shrine building measuring 2 shaku 5 sun in depth. It was a shrine enshrined during the era of Emperor Keikō, with Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto as the two main deities, and Koyō Myōjin as an accompanying deity. In Bunka 12 (1815), the lord of the Sōma Domain, {{ill|lt=Sōma Masutane|Q8514605|WD=Q8514605}}, ordered shrine priests and a man named Watanabe Yoshitsuna to investigate and identify the eight Shikinaisha in Namekata District. Watanabe Yoshitsuna reported that the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha known as Taka Shrine was the Taka Daimyōjin enshrined within the grounds of Kōmyō-ji Temple in Taka Village. In response, Takadama Tanba, a shrine priest of the Kibune Shrine in Odaka Village, submitted a counter-report claiming that Taka Shrine was a small shrine enshrined in Odaka Village. According to that report, Taka Shrine was a shrine where the Yasakani no Magatama, one of the Three Sacred Treasures, was enshrined as the local tutelary deity, and it had long been the guardian deity of Odaka Village. The ancestors of the shrine priest family originally bore the name “Takadama,” but out of deference to Taka Shrine they changed it to “Takadama.” The Taga faith’s head shrine, {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091}} in Shiga Prefecture, is called “O-Taga-sama,” and the name Odaka is said to derive from calling the enshrined Taka Shrine “Mi-Taka.” The Takadama family administered the rites of both Kifune Shrine and Taka Shrine, which were enshrined near {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōma Odaka Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in Odaka Ward. Taka Shrine was located at a place called Nihonmatsu in Odaka Village, about ten ken from Kifune Shrine. Later, Taka Shrine was washed away by a flood during the Genroku era, but a farmer recovered the small shrine from the water and it was enshrined together within the precincts of Kifune Shrine. Takadama Tanba wrote down the above origins of Taka Shrine in Odaka Village and reported them to the domain. However, according to the compiler of *Ōsōshi*: * In shrine identification surveys conducted before the domain’s Bunka 12 investigation, Taka Shrine of Odaka Village is not recorded. * Records of the Shima family, shrine priests of Taka Shrine in Taka Village, appear in shrine priest registries from Genroku 15 and in temple and shrine surveys from the Shōtoku era, whereas records of the Takadama family only appear from Kanpō 1 onward. For these reasons, the theory of Takadama Tanba is said to contain many doubtful points. At present, the inscription on the stone monument describing the shrine’s origins at the entrance of Kifune Shrine’s approach states that the enshrined deities are Takaokami-no-Kami and Yamiokami-no-Kami, and that Taka Shrine is enshrined together in the same sanctuary. === Shrine Grounds === File:Precincts of Kifune-jinja,Minamisoma.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Haiden of Kifune-jinja after massive earthquake 11 March 2011.JPG|Worship Hall File:Honden of Kifune-jinja after massive earthquake 11 March 2011.JPG|Main Hall
Nagare-zukuri style. === Related Sites === In the Sōma region, there are said to be five Taka Shrines in total, including the two shrines described above. File:Taka-jinja shrine at Suzumigaoka Hachiman-jinja shrine.JPG|Taka Shrine, a precinct shrine within Suzumigaoka Hachiman Shrine in Sōma City === On-site Information === '''Locations''' * Taka Shrine: {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}112 Takajō-no-uchi * Kifune Shrine: {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Odaka Ward|Q24892654|WD=Q24892654}}2-50 Nakamachi === References === * Guide booklet for visiting Taka Shrine (Taka Shrine Office) * *Ōsōshi (History of Sōma City, Vol. 4)* (Sōma City, 1969) === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External Links === * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/276003.html Taka Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Compilation”) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Taka Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Taka Shrine (Minamisōma)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Taka Shrine (Minamisōma)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:南相馬市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社|名たかしんしや]] [[Category:福島県の旧県社]] [[Category:南相馬市の建築物]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

{{wikidata link|Q11430895}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Taka Shrine |Image = [[File:Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine in Shinto ritual season 1.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}112 Takajō-no-uchi |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2 DMS|37|36|2.81|N|140|59|33.52|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=ti|name=Taka Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} (Taka Ara-Mitama-no-Mikoto) |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = 40th year of Emperor Keikō (12th Emperor), 7th month |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = Taka Daimyōjin |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Taka Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taka Shrine''' (たかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank}} it was designated as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined Deity === * '''{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}''' *: According to *Ōsōshi*, a local historical record of the Sōma Domain, the shrine enshrines “Taka Ara-Mitama-no-Mikoto,” the {{ill|lt=Ara-mitama and Nigi-mitama|Q495568|WD=Q495568|lt=Ara-mitama}} (rough spirit) of Izanagi-no-Mikoto. The shintai (sacred object) has been prohibited from being viewed since ancient times and is therefore unknown. There is a theory that it may be a “hawk statue,” but this hawk statue is said to be the result of a mistaken transmission identifying the “White-Token Hawk” mentioned in shrine tradition as the shintai. === History === === Overview === Taka Shrine is the foremost of the eight {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Namekata District, Fukushima|Q11626139|WD=Q11626139}}, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389}} (Taka Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}}, {{ill|lt=Mitachi Shrine|Q11488802|WD=Q11488802}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masutamine Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himatsuri Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takakura Shrine|Q11669904|WD=Q11669904}}, {{ill|lt=Kanmuri Mine Shrine|Q11395049|WD=Q11395049}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oshio Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}). Among shrines bearing the name “Taga Shrine,” it is the only one nationwide to have been granted the rank of {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}, and it is said that Taga Shrines throughout the Tōhoku region originate from this shrine. Although its exact origins are unknown, tradition holds that the shrine was founded in the 40th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q461258}}, who visited this area during his eastern campaign and enshrined the deity to pray for victory in battle. To the northwest of the current location lies the Furuuchi area, through which the Ōta River flows, and there is said to be a place called “Daimyōjin Kawara.” The ancient shrine site is said to have been on the southern hills of Daimyōjin Kawara. In the 7th year, 2nd month of {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}, a violent storm washed away and severely damaged the shrine buildings, which drifted to the banks of the nearby Ōta River. In the 9th month of the same year, the shrine was relocated to Ashinodaira (present-day Jō-no-uchi, the current site). In Kōryaku 2 ({{ill|lt=1380|Q6327|WD=Q6327}}), there existed a bronze bell (waniguchi) bearing an inscription of donation by Fujiwara Yoshimori, which is said to hang in the inner sanctuary of the main hall. From April of Jōō 4, Kinoe-mi year ({{ill|lt=1655|Q6975|WD=Q6975}}) to Meireki 1 (the same year), the shrine sanctuary was repaired, with timber donated by {{ill|lt=Sōma Tadatane|Q8514579|WD=Q8514579}}. During the {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} era, {{ill|lt=Sōma Masatane|Q8514599|WD=Q8514599}} built a sanctuary called “Yōshinden,” associated with {{ill|lt=Yoshida Shintō|Q1760351|WD=Q1760351}}, within the castle grounds, and at that time, for certain reasons, had a statue of the “White-Token Hawk” carved and dedicated to Taka Shrine. This wooden statue of the “White-Token Hawk” is said to have been stored in a miniature shrine. Even today, the “White-Token Hawk Statue” is enshrined in the main hall as a sacred treasure. According to shrine tradition, although it appears to be an ordinary wooden statue, it was named “White-Token Hawk” because the feathers appear white depending on how the light strikes them. In Kyōhō 9 ({{ill|lt=1724|Q6926|WD=Q6926}}), Sōma Masatane donated shrine lands amounting to 1 koku and 9 to, among other offerings, and the shrine received deep reverence from successive lords of the Sōma Domain. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was designated a village shrine, and in Shōwa 19 ({{ill|lt=1944|Q5268|WD=Q5268}}), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine. Taka Shrine has long been believed to bestow divine benefits such as longevity, fulfillment of wishes, household safety, safe childbirth, maritime safety, and abundant catches. Because it was originally enshrined to pray for victory in battle, many worshippers in particular visit to pray for certain victory in elections. === Court Rank === * Enryaku 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452}}) 10th month, 22nd day: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Kōki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}*) – recorded as “Taga Shrine.” === Shrine Grounds === === Shrine Buildings === * Main Hall – Built in one-bay nagare-zukuri style, with side screens and balustrades. Dragon carvings are applied to the side screens. File:Honden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Main Hall File:Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine in Shinto ritual season 2.JPG|Worship Hall '''Interior of the Heiden''' The coffered lattice ceiling of the heiden is decorated with polychrome paintings of animals, plants, and human figures. Only during the first three days of the New Year, a goma altar is set up in front of the main hall doors within the heiden, where goma fire rituals are performed. During these rituals, prayers are offered by holding amulets and votive items directly over the sacred flames. File:Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Interior of the Heiden File:Pictures of Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Paintings on the lattice ceiling of the Heiden
The rear of the photo shows the side of the main hall doors. File:Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Paintings on the lattice ceiling of the Heiden
The rear of the photo shows the Heiden entrance. File:Gomadan of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Goma altar at Taka Shrine
Only during the New Year period, a tree called “Nodeppō” is used as goma sticks for the ritual. '''Interior of the Worship Hall''' Inside the worship hall are housed items such as an ema depicting “Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Izanami-no-Mikoto,” paintings of hawks, and a plaque reading “Protection of the State,” dedicated in Meiji 38 to commemorate the {{ill|lt=Siege of Port Arthur|Q210800|WD=Q210800|lt=Fall of Port Arthur}} during the Russo-Japanese War. File:Inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine 1.JPG|Interior of the Worship Hall File:Inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Interior of the Worship Hall / Entrance to the Heiden File:Ema board of Izanagi and Izanami at Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Ema of Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Izanami-no-Mikoto File:Picture of Falcon inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Hawk painting
Many hawk images are dedicated because the shrine is also called “Taka Daimyōjin.” File:Board of "spiritual protection of the state" at Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|“Protection of the State” votive plaque === Other Structures === * Stone statues of the Seven Lucky Gods * Near the sacred tree behind the main hall, Buddhist statues from the era of syncretism between Shinto and Buddhism are placed. === Approach === Along the approach is a sacred pond, and on the small central island stand a small shrine of Kogota Yamatsumi Shrine and a stone monument of Konpira Shrine. The first torii gate stands southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Ōta Station|Q6100384|WD=Q6100384}}, along the tracks of the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263}}. File:Kogota Yamatsumi-jinja shrine and Konpira-jinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Small shrine of Kogota Yamatsumi Shrine and stone monument of Konpira Shrine File:Main approach to Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Approach File:Main approach to Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Entrance to the approach of Taka Shrine File:Torii gate of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|First torii gate === Subsidiary and Branch Shrines === * Yasaka-Inari Shrine *: The shrine building reuses the former main hall of Taka Shrine, and within it are enshrined Yasaka Shrine and Inari Shrine. Yasaka Shrine was formerly called Gozu Tennō Shrine and had been located in the Jō-no-uchi area, the present location. According to *Ōsōshi*, the sacred object of Yasaka Shrine measures 7 sun in length. Inari Shrine was formerly located in the Oinari area southeast of the current site and was later enshrined together with Yasaka Shrine. *: On the east side of the shrine building are a sacred cedar tree, Raijin Shrine, and a stone monument of Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine). * Raijin Shrine *: The sacred object is recorded as measuring 7 sun in length. * Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine) Stone Monument *: The enshrined deities are Amaterasu Ōmikami, Tsukuyomi-no-Kami, and Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami. It was transferred from {{ill|lt=Aomasa Shrine|Q11662833|WD=Q11662833}} in Iwakiri, Miyagino Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture. File:Yasaka-Inari-jinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Yasaka-Inari Shrine (former main hall of Taka Shrine) File:Sanctified Japanese cedar tree and raijinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Sacred tree and Raijin Shrine File:Raijinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Raijin Shrine File:Aoso-jinja shrine in taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone monument of Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine) File:Statues of Shichifukujin in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone statues of the Seven Lucky Gods File:Sanctified tree and Statue of Kannon in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Sacred tree and Buddhist statue === Kifune Shrine (Ronsha of Taka Shrine) === {{Shrine |Name = Kifune Shrine |Image = [[File:Haiden of Kifune-jinja shrine in Odaka ward,Minamisouma city. 01.JPG|270px]]

{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} (trad.)
{{ill|lt=Takami-Musubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} (trad.)
Koyō Myōjin (trad.) |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} |Founded = Unknown (before Genkō 3 ({{ill|lt=1323|Q5771|WD=Q5771}})) |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Annual festival = Second Sunday of January |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kifune Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E|}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=290|coord={{coord|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E}}}} }} It is enshrined near Sōma Odaka Shrine in Odaka Ward, Minamisōma City. The enshrined deities are Takaokami-no-Kami and Yamiokami-no-Kami. === History === The founding date of Kifune Shrine is unknown, but it is said to have been worshipped since before Genkō 3 (1323). According to *Ōsōshi*, there is a record stating that the box housing the sacred object bears an inscription indicating it was made in the 5th year of Hōen, kinoe-mi year, 8th month. The sacred object is said to be a statue “riding a dragon and wearing white robes.” In Bunji 5, when {{ill|lt=Sōma Moritsune|Q11582495|WD=Q11582495}} took control of Namekata District, he designated Kifune Shrine as the district’s chief shrine and deeply revered it, carrying out embellishments of the shrine buildings. Kifune Shrine was mainly worshipped as a deity of rainmaking and stopping rain, bountiful harvests, success and advancement in life, and martial arts. According to *Ōsōshi*, Taka Shrine once existed within the precincts of Kibune (Kifune) Shrine in Minami-Odaka Village. It was a shrine building measuring 2 shaku 5 sun in depth. It was a shrine enshrined during the era of Emperor Keikō, with Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto as the two main deities, and Koyō Myōjin as an accompanying deity. In Bunka 12 (1815), the lord of the Sōma Domain, {{ill|lt=Sōma Masutane|Q8514605|WD=Q8514605}}, ordered shrine priests and a man named Watanabe Yoshitsuna to investigate and identify the eight Shikinaisha in Namekata District. Watanabe Yoshitsuna reported that the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha known as Taka Shrine was the Taka Daimyōjin enshrined within the grounds of Kōmyō-ji Temple in Taka Village. In response, Takadama Tanba, a shrine priest of the Kibune Shrine in Odaka Village, submitted a counter-report claiming that Taka Shrine was a small shrine enshrined in Odaka Village. According to that report, Taka Shrine was a shrine where the Yasakani no Magatama, one of the Three Sacred Treasures, was enshrined as the local tutelary deity, and it had long been the guardian deity of Odaka Village. The ancestors of the shrine priest family originally bore the name “Takadama,” but out of deference to Taka Shrine they changed it to “Takadama.” The Taga faith’s head shrine, {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091}} in Shiga Prefecture, is called “O-Taga-sama,” and the name Odaka is said to derive from calling the enshrined Taka Shrine “Mi-Taka.” The Takadama family administered the rites of both Kifune Shrine and Taka Shrine, which were enshrined near {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōma Odaka Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in Odaka Ward. Taka Shrine was located at a place called Nihonmatsu in Odaka Village, about ten ken from Kifune Shrine. Later, Taka Shrine was washed away by a flood during the Genroku era, but a farmer recovered the small shrine from the water and it was enshrined together within the precincts of Kifune Shrine. Takadama Tanba wrote down the above origins of Taka Shrine in Odaka Village and reported them to the domain. However, according to the compiler of *Ōsōshi*: * In shrine identification surveys conducted before the domain’s Bunka 12 investigation, Taka Shrine of Odaka Village is not recorded. * Records of the Shima family, shrine priests of Taka Shrine in Taka Village, appear in shrine priest registries from Genroku 15 and in temple and shrine surveys from the Shōtoku era, whereas records of the Takadama family only appear from Kanpō 1 onward. For these reasons, the theory of Takadama Tanba is said to contain many doubtful points. At present, the inscription on the stone monument describing the shrine’s origins at the entrance of Kifune Shrine’s approach states that the enshrined deities are Takaokami-no-Kami and Yamiokami-no-Kami, and that Taka Shrine is enshrined together in the same sanctuary. === Shrine Grounds === File:Precincts of Kifune-jinja,Minamisoma.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Haiden of Kifune-jinja after massive earthquake 11 March 2011.JPG|Worship Hall File:Honden of Kifune-jinja after massive earthquake 11 March 2011.JPG|Main Hall
Nagare-zukuri style. === Related Sites === In the Sōma region, there are said to be five Taka Shrines in total, including the two shrines described above. File:Taka-jinja shrine at Suzumigaoka Hachiman-jinja shrine.JPG|Taka Shrine, a precinct shrine within Suzumigaoka Hachiman Shrine in Sōma City === On-site Information === '''Locations''' * Taka Shrine: {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679}}112 Takajō-no-uchi * Kifune Shrine: {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Odaka Ward|Q24892654|WD=Q24892654}}2-50 Nakamachi === References === * Guide booklet for visiting Taka Shrine (Taka Shrine Office) * *Ōsōshi (History of Sōma City, Vol. 4)* (Sōma City, 1969) === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External Links === * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/276003.html Taka Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Compilation”) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Taka Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Taka Shrine (Minamisōma)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Taka Shrine (Minamisōma)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:南相馬市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社|名たかしんしや]] [[Category:福島県の旧県社]] [[Category:南相馬市の建築物]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

(Q135040020)}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県南相馬市原町区高城ノ内112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Taka-jinja}} [[JA:多珂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430895}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Taka Shrine |Image = [[File:Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine in Shinto ritual season 1.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679|1=Haramachi-ku}}112 Takajō-no-uchi |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2 DMS|37|36|2.81|N|140|59|33.52|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=ti|name=Taka Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} (Taka Ara-Mitama-no-Mikoto) |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = 40th year of Emperor Keikō (12th Emperor), 7th month |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Taka Daimyōjin |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Taka Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taka Shrine''' (たかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679|1=Haramachi-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined Deity === * '''{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}''' *: According to *Ōsōshi*, a local historical record of the Sōma Domain, the shrine enshrines “Taka Ara-Mitama-no-Mikoto,” the {{ill|lt=Ara-mitama and Nigi-mitama|Q495568|WD=Q495568|lt=Ara-mitama|1=Mitama}} (rough spirit) of Izanagi-no-Mikoto. The shintai (sacred object) has been prohibited from being viewed since ancient times and is therefore unknown. There is a theory that it may be a “hawk statue,” but this hawk statue is said to be the result of a mistaken transmission identifying the “White-Token Hawk” mentioned in shrine tradition as the shintai. === History === === Overview === Taka Shrine is the foremost of the eight {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Namekata District, Fukushima|Q11626139|WD=Q11626139|1=Namekata district}}, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} (Taka Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124|1=Kashima Miko Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Mitachi Shrine|Q11488802|WD=Q11488802|1=Mito Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masutamine Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himatsuri Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takakura Shrine|Q11669904|WD=Q11669904|1=Takakura Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Kanmuri Mine Shrine|Q11395049|WD=Q11395049|1=Sakamine Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oshio Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}). Among shrines bearing the name “Taga Shrine,” it is the only one nationwide to have been granted the rank of {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, and it is said that Taga Shrines throughout the Tōhoku region originate from this shrine. Although its exact origins are unknown, tradition holds that the shrine was founded in the 40th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}}, who visited this area during his eastern campaign and enshrined the deity to pray for victory in battle. To the northwest of the current location lies the Furuuchi area, through which the Ōta River flows, and there is said to be a place called “Daimyōjin Kawara.” The ancient shrine site is said to have been on the southern hills of Daimyōjin Kawara. In the 7th year, 2nd month of {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}, a violent storm washed away and severely damaged the shrine buildings, which drifted to the banks of the nearby Ōta River. In the 9th month of the same year, the shrine was relocated to Ashinodaira (present-day Jō-no-uchi, the current site). In Kōryaku 2 ({{ill|lt=1380|Q6327|WD=Q6327|1=1380}}), there existed a bronze bell (waniguchi) bearing an inscription of donation by Fujiwara Yoshimori, which is said to hang in the inner sanctuary of the main hall. From April of Jōō 4, Kinoe-mi year ({{ill|lt=1655|Q6975|WD=Q6975|1=1655}}) to Meireki 1 (the same year), the shrine sanctuary was repaired, with timber donated by {{ill|lt=Sōma Tadatane|Q8514579|WD=Q8514579|1=Sōma Tadatane}}. During the {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} era, {{ill|lt=Sōma Masatane|Q8514599|WD=Q8514599|1=Sōma Masatane}} built a sanctuary called “Yōshinden,” associated with {{ill|lt=Yoshida Shintō|Q1760351|WD=Q1760351|1=Yoshida Shintō}}, within the castle grounds, and at that time, for certain reasons, had a statue of the “White-Token Hawk” carved and dedicated to Taka Shrine. This wooden statue of the “White-Token Hawk” is said to have been stored in a miniature shrine. Even today, the “White-Token Hawk Statue” is enshrined in the main hall as a sacred treasure. According to shrine tradition, although it appears to be an ordinary wooden statue, it was named “White-Token Hawk” because the feathers appear white depending on how the light strikes them. In Kyōhō 9 ({{ill|lt=1724|Q6926|WD=Q6926|1=1724}}), Sōma Masatane donated shrine lands amounting to 1 koku and 9 to, among other offerings, and the shrine received deep reverence from successive lords of the Sōma Domain. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was designated a village shrine, and in Shōwa 19 ({{ill|lt=1944|Q5268|WD=Q5268|1=1944}}), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine. Taka Shrine has long been believed to bestow divine benefits such as longevity, fulfillment of wishes, household safety, safe childbirth, maritime safety, and abundant catches. Because it was originally enshrined to pray for victory in battle, many worshippers in particular visit to pray for certain victory in elections. === Court Rank === * Enryaku 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}) 10th month, 22nd day: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Kōki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon Kōki}}*) – recorded as “Taga Shrine.” === Shrine Grounds === === Shrine Buildings === * Main Hall – Built in one-bay nagare-zukuri style, with side screens and balustrades. Dragon carvings are applied to the side screens. File:Honden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Main Hall File:Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine in Shinto ritual season 2.JPG|Worship Hall '''Interior of the Heiden''' The coffered lattice ceiling of the heiden is decorated with polychrome paintings of animals, plants, and human figures. Only during the first three days of the New Year, a goma altar is set up in front of the main hall doors within the heiden, where goma fire rituals are performed. During these rituals, prayers are offered by holding amulets and votive items directly over the sacred flames. File:Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Interior of the Heiden File:Pictures of Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Paintings on the lattice ceiling of the Heiden
The rear of the photo shows the side of the main hall doors. File:Heiden of Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Paintings on the lattice ceiling of the Heiden
The rear of the photo shows the Heiden entrance. File:Gomadan of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Goma altar at Taka Shrine
Only during the New Year period, a tree called “Nodeppō” is used as goma sticks for the ritual. '''Interior of the Worship Hall''' Inside the worship hall are housed items such as an ema depicting “Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Izanami-no-Mikoto,” paintings of hawks, and a plaque reading “Protection of the State,” dedicated in Meiji 38 to commemorate the {{ill|lt=Siege of Port Arthur|Q210800|WD=Q210800|lt=Fall of Port Arthur|1=Siege of Port Arthur}} during the Russo-Japanese War. File:Inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine 1.JPG|Interior of the Worship Hall File:Inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Interior of the Worship Hall / Entrance to the Heiden File:Ema board of Izanagi and Izanami at Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Ema of Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Izanami-no-Mikoto File:Picture of Falcon inside of Haiden of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Hawk painting
Many hawk images are dedicated because the shrine is also called “Taka Daimyōjin.” File:Board of "spiritual protection of the state" at Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|“Protection of the State” votive plaque === Other Structures === * Stone statues of the Seven Lucky Gods * Near the sacred tree behind the main hall, Buddhist statues from the era of syncretism between Shinto and Buddhism are placed. === Approach === Along the approach is a sacred pond, and on the small central island stand a small shrine of Kogota Yamatsumi Shrine and a stone monument of Konpira Shrine. The first torii gate stands southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Ōta Station|Q6100384|WD=Q6100384|1=Iwaki-Ōta Station}}, along the tracks of the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263|1=Jōban Line}}. File:Kogota Yamatsumi-jinja shrine and Konpira-jinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Small shrine of Kogota Yamatsumi Shrine and stone monument of Konpira Shrine File:Main approach to Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Approach File:Main approach to Taka-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Entrance to the approach of Taka Shrine File:Torii gate of Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|First torii gate === Subsidiary and Branch Shrines === * Yasaka-Inari Shrine *: The shrine building reuses the former main hall of Taka Shrine, and within it are enshrined Yasaka Shrine and Inari Shrine. Yasaka Shrine was formerly called Gozu Tennō Shrine and had been located in the Jō-no-uchi area, the present location. According to *Ōsōshi*, the sacred object of Yasaka Shrine measures 7 sun in length. Inari Shrine was formerly located in the Oinari area southeast of the current site and was later enshrined together with Yasaka Shrine. *: On the east side of the shrine building are a sacred cedar tree, Raijin Shrine, and a stone monument of Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine). * Raijin Shrine *: The sacred object is recorded as measuring 7 sun in length. * Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine) Stone Monument *: The enshrined deities are Amaterasu Ōmikami, Tsukuyomi-no-Kami, and Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami. It was transferred from {{ill|lt=Aomasa Shrine|Q11662833|WD=Q11662833|1=Aoso Shrine}} in Iwakiri, Miyagino Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture. File:Yasaka-Inari-jinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Yasaka-Inari Shrine (former main hall of Taka Shrine) File:Sanctified Japanese cedar tree and raijinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Sacred tree and Raijin Shrine File:Raijinja shrine in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Raijin Shrine File:Aoso-jinja shrine in taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone monument of Aomasa Daigongen (Aomasa Shrine) File:Statues of Shichifukujin in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone statues of the Seven Lucky Gods File:Sanctified tree and Statue of Kannon in Taka-jinja shrine.JPG|Sacred tree and Buddhist statue === Kifune Shrine (Ronsha of Taka Shrine) === {{Shrine |Name = Kifune Shrine |Image = [[File:Haiden of Kifune-jinja shrine in Odaka ward,Minamisouma city. 01.JPG|270px]]
Worship Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Odaka Ward|Q24892654|WD=Q24892654|1=Odaka-ku}}2-50 Nakamachi |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2 DMS|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|name=Kifune Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami / Yamiokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}
{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} (trad.)
{{ill|lt=Takami-Musubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto|1=Takamimusubi}} (trad.)
Koyō Myōjin (trad.) |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} |Founded = Unknown (before Genkō 3 ({{ill|lt=1323|Q5771|WD=Q5771|1=1323}})) |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Annual festival = Second Sunday of January |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kifune Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E|}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=290|coord={{coord|37|33|59.4|N|140|59|23.5|E}}}} }} It is enshrined near Sōma Odaka Shrine in Odaka Ward, Minamisōma City. The enshrined deities are Takaokami-no-Kami and Yamiokami-no-Kami. === History === The founding date of Kifune Shrine is unknown, but it is said to have been worshipped since before Genkō 3 (1323). According to *Ōsōshi*, there is a record stating that the box housing the sacred object bears an inscription indicating it was made in the 5th year of Hōen, kinoe-mi year, 8th month. The sacred object is said to be a statue “riding a dragon and wearing white robes.” In Bunji 5, when {{ill|lt=Sōma Moritsune|Q11582495|WD=Q11582495|1=Sōma Moritsune}} took control of Namekata District, he designated Kifune Shrine as the district’s chief shrine and deeply revered it, carrying out embellishments of the shrine buildings. Kifune Shrine was mainly worshipped as a deity of rainmaking and stopping rain, bountiful harvests, success and advancement in life, and martial arts. According to *Ōsōshi*, Taka Shrine once existed within the precincts of Kibune (Kifune) Shrine in Minami-Odaka Village. It was a shrine building measuring 2 shaku 5 sun in depth. It was a shrine enshrined during the era of Emperor Keikō, with Izanagi-no-Mikoto and Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto as the two main deities, and Koyō Myōjin as an accompanying deity. In Bunka 12 (1815), the lord of the Sōma Domain, {{ill|lt=Sōma Masutane|Q8514605|WD=Q8514605|1=Sōma Masutane}}, ordered shrine priests and a man named Watanabe Yoshitsuna to investigate and identify the eight Shikinaisha in Namekata District. Watanabe Yoshitsuna reported that the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha known as Taka Shrine was the Taka Daimyōjin enshrined within the grounds of Kōmyō-ji Temple in Taka Village. In response, Takadama Tanba, a shrine priest of the Kibune Shrine in Odaka Village, submitted a counter-report claiming that Taka Shrine was a small shrine enshrined in Odaka Village. According to that report, Taka Shrine was a shrine where the Yasakani no Magatama, one of the Three Sacred Treasures, was enshrined as the local tutelary deity, and it had long been the guardian deity of Odaka Village. The ancestors of the shrine priest family originally bore the name “Takadama,” but out of deference to Taka Shrine they changed it to “Takadama.” The Taga faith’s head shrine, {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in Shiga Prefecture, is called “O-Taga-sama,” and the name Odaka is said to derive from calling the enshrined Taka Shrine “Mi-Taka.” The Takadama family administered the rites of both Kifune Shrine and Taka Shrine, which were enshrined near {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōma Odaka Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in Odaka Ward. Taka Shrine was located at a place called Nihonmatsu in Odaka Village, about ten ken from Kifune Shrine. Later, Taka Shrine was washed away by a flood during the Genroku era, but a farmer recovered the small shrine from the water and it was enshrined together within the precincts of Kifune Shrine. Takadama Tanba wrote down the above origins of Taka Shrine in Odaka Village and reported them to the domain. However, according to the compiler of *Ōsōshi*: * In shrine identification surveys conducted before the domain’s Bunka 12 investigation, Taka Shrine of Odaka Village is not recorded. * Records of the Shima family, shrine priests of Taka Shrine in Taka Village, appear in shrine priest registries from Genroku 15 and in temple and shrine surveys from the Shōtoku era, whereas records of the Takadama family only appear from Kanpō 1 onward. For these reasons, the theory of Takadama Tanba is said to contain many doubtful points. At present, the inscription on the stone monument describing the shrine’s origins at the entrance of Kifune Shrine’s approach states that the enshrined deities are Takaokami-no-Kami and Yamiokami-no-Kami, and that Taka Shrine is enshrined together in the same sanctuary. === Shrine Grounds === File:Precincts of Kifune-jinja,Minamisoma.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Haiden of Kifune-jinja after massive earthquake 11 March 2011.JPG|Worship Hall File:Honden of Kifune-jinja after massive earthquake 11 March 2011.JPG|Main Hall
Nagare-zukuri style. === Related Sites === In the Sōma region, there are said to be five Taka Shrines in total, including the two shrines described above. File:Taka-jinja shrine at Suzumigaoka Hachiman-jinja shrine.JPG|Taka Shrine, a precinct shrine within Suzumigaoka Hachiman Shrine in Sōma City === On-site Information === '''Locations''' * Taka Shrine: {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Haramachi Ward|Q24892679|WD=Q24892679|1=Haramachi-ku}}112 Takajō-no-uchi * Kifune Shrine: {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma City|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Odaka Ward|Q24892654|WD=Q24892654|1=Odaka-ku}}2-50 Nakamachi === References === * Guide booklet for visiting Taka Shrine (Taka Shrine Office) * *Ōsōshi (History of Sōma City, Vol. 4)* (Sōma City, 1969) === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External Links === * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/276003.html Taka Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Compilation”) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Taka Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Taka Shrine (Minamisōma)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Taka Shrine (Minamisōma)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:南相馬市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社|名たかしんしや]] [[Category:福島県の旧県社]] [[Category:南相馬市の建築物]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

It is located in [[Mutsu Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická
